questions.scenario: Staging passed with checkout-api at digest sha256:8f3a. The production job checks out the same commit but rebuilds the image, producing sha256:9b7c after a base image update. Which production action preserves the meaning of the staging evidence?

options.text: Deploy sha256:9b7c because the source commit matches.
Run a shortened production smoke test against sha256:9b7c because the source commit already passed staging.
Deploy sha256:8f3a, the exact artifact that passed staging.
Compare the package manifests and deploy sha256:9b7c if application files match sha256:8f3a.

questions.explanation: Source identity is not artifact identity. Rebuilding can change dependencies or build inputs even from the same commit. Promoting sha256:8f3a keeps production tied to the artifact that earned the evidence. See the [OCI image descriptor specification](https://github.com/opencontainers/image-spec/blob/main/descriptor.md).

questions.scenario: The same digest must run in staging and production, but the environments use different databases, payment endpoints, and secrets. Which design keeps both requirements true?

options.text: Build environment-neutral code once and inject environment-specific config and secret references at runtime.
Create a production image by adding only environment configuration to the staging image in a second layer.
Promote the same digest but rewrite its image tag to include the target database and payment endpoint.
Rebuild for each environment and compare only the source commit.

questions.explanation: The artifact should stay immutable while the runtime supplies environment-specific values. Baking secrets creates a different artifact and spreads sensitive data, while sharing production resources with staging destroys the environment boundary. See [The Twelve-Factor App: Config](https://12factor.net/config).

questions.scenario: The workflow graph is build, deploy-staging, and deploy-production. Production depends only on build, so it can start even when staging smoke tests fail. What is the correct gate relationship?

options.text: Production should depend on the staging deployment job, but not its smoke-test result, because approval evaluates that evidence.
Production should require both build and manual approval, while staging verification remains informational.
Production should depend on a successful staging run for the commit, even if staging deployed a different rebuilt digest.
Production should depend on successful staging deployment and verification for the same artifact.

questions.explanation: A promotion gate is useful only if failure blocks the next environment. Human approval is an additional decision point, not a substitute for staging evidence. Parallel production bypasses the trust chain. See [GitHub Actions workflow syntax](https://docs.github.com/en/actions/reference/workflows-and-actions/workflow-syntax).

questions.scenario: A production reviewer sees only the commit message and an Approve button. The release changes checkout pricing and uses a canary. Which approval context would support a real decision?

options.text: The pull request approval, commit identifier, and current production image tag.
Artifact digest, staging and scan evidence, rollout plan, data risk, rollback target, and watch signals.
The successful staging workflow URL and canary percentage, without artifact or recovery details.
The artifact digest and scan result, but no database-risk assessment or rollback target.

questions.explanation: Approval needs the identity of the artifact plus evidence and recovery context. A commit message or mutable tag does not tell the reviewer what will run, what passed, or how the team will stop. See [Reviewing GitHub Actions deployments](https://docs.github.com/en/actions/how-tos/deploy/configure-and-manage-deployments/review-deployments).

questions.scenario: The production tag currently points to sha256:8f3a. A second release moves the tag to sha256:9b7c. Six hours later, an incident responder asks what version handled an affected order. Which record is most useful?

options.text: The current value of the production tag.
The commit associated with the image tag at the time the incident is investigated.
A release record with time, digest, provenance, config, and approver.
A deployment log that records the mutable tag, environment, and approver but not the resolved digest.

questions.explanation: Tags move, so their current value cannot reconstruct historical deployment state. A release record connects immutable content to source, build, configuration, approval, and time. See [SLSA provenance](https://slsa.dev/spec/v1.0/provenance).

questions.scenario: A checkout-api incident began when two production jobs changed traffic concurrently and the later job rebuilt the application instead of promoting the digest that passed staging. The team must prevent another overlap, preserve artifact identity, and leave an accountable recovery record. Which controls belong in the redesigned promotion path?

options.text: Target a protected production environment with accountable reviewers.
Rebuild from the default branch immediately before deployment.
Use a concurrency group that prevents overlapping checkout-api production deployments.
Deploy the exact digest recorded by the successful staging job.
Record the runtime config version and previous rollback target.
Allow parallel production jobs when they deploy different commits, but resolve tag conflicts after completion.

questions.explanation: Environment protection, serialized production changes, immutable artifact promotion, and config or rollback traceability form a coherent gate. Rebuilding breaks the evidence chain. See [GitHub Actions environments](https://docs.github.com/en/actions/how-tos/deploy/configure-and-manage-deployments/manage-environments) and [workflow concurrency](https://docs.github.com/en/actions/how-tos/write-workflows/choose-when-workflows-run/control-workflow-concurrency).
